elements.sent-2411-010: In recent decades, procurement agencies across the world have come under increasing pressure to improve performance and deliver projects faster.
elements.sent-2411-011: The reason is that the traditional approach, when the contract is awarded to the lowest price bidder, fails to capture the trade-off between costs and quality of procurement.
elements.sent-2411-012: At the same time, quality may represent a large portion of the buyer's utility.
elements.sent-2411-013: As a result, numerous alternative auction designs have emerged, see \cite{molenaar2007alternative for an overview.}
elements.sent-2411-014: One such design is the scoring auction, where the contract is awarded to the firm with the best combination of price and quality.
elements.sent-2411-015: It was shown to be superior to the traditional approach theoretically (see \cite{che1993design,asker2008properties,asker2010procurement ) when quality is contractible, and the associated costs are winner-pay.}
elements.sent-2411-016: A special case when quality is represented by the speed of delivery is known as ``A+B auctions'' in road construction (see \cite{lewis2011procurement ).}

elements.sent-2411-309: The nature of equilibrium supporting our mechanism is that of semi-symmetric and semi-separating.

elements.sent-2411-314: We can speculate that for the environments that are neither sufficiently convex nor sufficiently concave, the optimal mechanism would feature ranges of symmetric and asymmetric behavior separated by multiple thresholds and multiple side payments.
elements.sent-2411-315: Given the pooling of types at a score floor or ceiling, it is only natural that the transfer experiences a discontinuity (our side payment) at the threshold type to align the IC constraints around the threshold.
elements.sent-2411-316: Without the discontinuity in transfers, the equilibrium utility would be discontinuous due to a sudden change in the probability of winning, so the types next to the threshold would surely deviate.
elements.sent-2411-317: In particular, suppose there was no required kickback in the score ceiling mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-318: In this case, the types of the favored bidder just to the right of the threshold would opt to win without competition, making the outcome more asymmetric than desired.
elements.sent-2411-319: The kickback ensures that the types to the right of the threshold instead choose to compete with the other bidder fairly, thus maintaining exactly the desired degree of outcome symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-320: It is worth mentioning that score floor and score ceiling mechanisms are not just mirror images of each other.
elements.sent-2411-321: Regardless of the mechanism, every type's equilibrium profit (informational rent) depends on the allocation to all worse types but not better types.
elements.sent-2411-322: This means that the modifications needed to create an asymmetry among the best and among the worst types will be different.
elements.sent-2411-323: To create an asymmetry among the best types without affecting the worst (rightmost) types, one only has to adjust the classical auction design ``for the strong bidders'', so the side payment in the score floor is only paid by the firm that won.
elements.sent-2411-324: A naive attempt to similarly create asymmetry among the worst (rightmost) types would inadvertently interfere with the IC constraints for the best types.
elements.sent-2411-325: Thus, the proper adjustment should affect ``both weak and strong bidders''.
elements.sent-2411-326: As a result, the side payment in the score floor auction, unlike the one in the score ceiling auction, has to be paid regardless of winning.

elements.sent-2411-370: In this paper, we considered the problem of optimal procurement in the presence of both winner-pay (production) and all-pay (investment) costs, and contractible quality.
elements.sent-2411-371: For this, we built a special mechanism design framework in which outcomes can be conditioned on both messages and qualities.
elements.sent-2411-372: In this framework, we first characterized optimal symmetric mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-373: We show that an optimal symmetric mechanism is a scoring auction with a scoring rule that is either flatter or steeper than the well-known rule obtained under assumption of winner-pay costs only \citep{che1993design .}
elements.sent-2411-374: Crucially, even with ex ante symmetric firms, an optimal mechanism may be asymmetric, i.e., exhibit favoritism.
elements.sent-2411-375: This happens when the importance of private information is relatively low or the elasticity of investment costs with respect to quality is sufficiently high.
elements.sent-2411-376: We characterized optimal mechanisms without the symmetry restriction which can be either sole sourcing, the symmetric scoring auction, or, depending on the curvature of marginal investment costs, a certain combination of the two.
elements.sent-2411-377: We identify two combinations that may be optimal.
elements.sent-2411-378: One involves a discriminating treatment of relatively inefficient types of two firms (a score floor auction) while the other discriminates between relatively efficient types of two firms (a score ceiling auction).
elements.sent-2411-379: Both involve side-payments that are needed to ensure the correct incentives of the favored bidder.
elements.sent-2411-380: It is hard to ignore that our mechanisms resemble corruption.
elements.sent-2411-381: Indeed, in the score ceiling auction, the side payment is paid to the auctioneer whenever the favored firm is guaranteed to win.
elements.sent-2411-382: Moreover, even our environment resembles those that are commonly associated with corruption: large investment costs and vanishing private information.
elements.sent-2411-383: But they are, in fact, quite different.
elements.sent-2411-384: Favoritism is efficient here, because it reduces the duplication of investment costs, and the auctioneer can not be bribed, as he is also the buyer.
elements.sent-2411-385: Thus, favoritism is not necessarily a sign of corruption.
elements.sent-2411-386: To conclude, it is well known that, in a procurement auction, buyer sometimes tries to give an advantage to his preferred bidder, not because he is corrupt, but because he believes that this bidder would do the best job.
elements.sent-2411-387: One can speculate that introducing asymmetric mechanisms into the procurement code may be a way to legitimize such favoritism.
